coredata.dc:title: Room temperature weakly ferromagnetic energy band opened graphene quantum dot coupled solid sheets – A possible carbon based dilute magnetic semiconductor
coredata.dc:creator: Bharathi, Ganapathi
Nataraj, Devaraj
Yu Khyzhun, Oleg
Thangadurai, Daniel T.
Senthilkumar, Kittusamy
Sowmiya, Murugaiyan
Kathiresan, Ramasamy
Kolandaivel, Ponmalai
Gupta, Mukul
Phase, Deodatta
Patra, Nirmalendu
Nath Jha, Shambhu
Bhattacharyya, Dibyendu
coredata.dc:description: To induce bandgap energy and magnetic properties is a challenging one in graphene. In the present work, we have prepared energy bandgap opened graphene system with room temperature weak ferromagnetic behavior in a hydrothermal process. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are interconnected through iron (Fe) metal atoms to form larger graphene solid sheets with induced energy bandgap. Unlike pristine graphene sheets which has zero energy bandgap, the as-formed graphene solid sheets are having intrinsic optical absorption and photoluminescence properties, due to its bandgap opened nature. In this interconnection process the quantum behavior of the GQDs is retained, even after becoming micron sized sheet like sample. Local structural discontinuity resulted around the metal atoms has helped to retain the GQD behavior in the solid sheet sample. At the same time the linker Fe atoms provided room temperature weak ferromagnetic behavior to the system. The present paper discusses the outcome of the characterization results, which demonstrate the energy bandgap opened nature and magnetic behavior of the GQD interconnected solid sheet system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene Quantum Dots
GQD solid sheets
FRET
Weak ferromagnetism
Photocurrent
Dilute magnetic semiconductor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Author GB acknowledges the University Grants Commission, Govt. of India, for providing BSR fellowship. Author DN acknowledges the University Grants Commission, Govt. of India, for sanctioning the centre for Advanced studies in physics for the Development of Solar Energy Materials and Devices (Award No. F. No. 2-1/2013(NS/PE)). DN also acknowledges RUSA Program Phase I (Component-8) Research and Innovation, India.

Author GB acknowledges the University Grants Commission, Govt. of India, for providing BSR fellowship. Author DN acknowledges the University Grants Commission, Govt. of India, for sanctioning the centre for Advanced studies in physics for the Development of Solar Energy Materials and Devices (Award No. F. No. 2-1/2013(NS/PE)). DN also acknowledges RUSA Program Phase I (Component-8) Research and Innovation, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GQDs, (b) GQDs prepared with 0.1 mole Fe, (c) GQD solid sheets prepared with 0.3 mole Fe (d) GQD solid sheets prepared with 0.5 mole Fe, (e) SAED pattern of GQDs and (f) SAED pattern of GQD solid sheets prepared with 0.5 mole Fe.

High-resolution XPS spectra of GQDs and GQD interconnected solid sheets measured at (a) C1s, (b) N1s, and (c) O1s positions. Representative deconvolution of high-resolution spectra measured at (d) C1s and (e) N1s. (f) Fe 2p high-resolution XPS spectra of GQD interconnected solid sheets.
Raman spectra measured from GQDs and GQD interconnected solid sheets.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectra and (b) PL emission spectra measured from GQDs and GQD interconnected solid sheet samples.
Schematic diagram representing the use of GQDs as FRET donor for exciting Fe interconnected solid sheets.
PL emission decay curves of Fe interconnected solid sheets (separated from F5 sample) and GQDs.
Soft X-ray absorption spectra measured at (a) C K-edge and (b) N K-edge from GQDs (F1) and GQD interconnected solid sheets.
(a) NEXAFS and (b) EXAFS spectra of Fe metal atom interconnected GQD solid sheets measured at Fe K-edge.
(a) k 2 weighted χ(k) spectra of Fe metal atom interconnected GQD solid sheets, (b) experimental χ(R) vs R data fitted with the theoretically generated plot at Fe k-edge of GQD solid sheets.
Chemical structure of Fe interconnected GQD solid sheets proposed based on the X-ray absorption measurements.
FTIR spectra of GQDs (F1) and GQD interconnected solid sheets.
M−H plot of GQDs and inset graph shows the enlarged hysteresis curve near zero magnetic field.
M−H plot of (a) F3, (b) F5, (c) F7, and (d) F9. Inset graphs show enlarged hysteresis curves of corresponding samples near zero magnetic field.
(a, b) Top and side views of graphene structure with Fe metal atom interacting at the top site and (c, d) Top and side views of graphene structure with Fe metal atom interacting at the hollow site.
(a) Top view of graphene structure with Fe metal atom interacting at the edge site and (b) Top view of graphene structure with Fe metal atom interacting at the edge site of two graphene sheets.

(a) & (b) FESEM images of electrophoretically deposited solid sheet sample (separated from F5) on FTO substrate; Photocurrent curves recorded from (c) Fe solid sheet film and (d) Fe solid sheet film over-coated with GQDs.
Values of coordination number (C.N), Bond length (R(Å)) and Debye-Waller factor (σ2 (Å2)) obtained from EXAFS analysis at Fe K-edge.

head.ce:title: Room temperature weakly ferromagnetic energy band opened graphene quantum dot coupled solid sheets – A possible carbon based dilute magnetic semiconductor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fig. (a) Schematic diagram representing the use of GQDs as FRET donor for exciting Fe interconnected solid sheets, (b) PL emission decay curves of Fe interconnected solid sheets and GQDs, (c) Photocurrent curves recorded from (c) Fe solid sheet film and (d) Fe solid sheet film over-coated with GQDs, (d) M−H plot of GQDs and GQD interconnected solid sheets.

To induce bandgap energy and magnetic properties is a challenging one in graphene. In the present work, we have prepared energy bandgap opened graphene system with room temperature weak ferromagnetic behavior in a hydrothermal process. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are interconnected through iron (Fe) metal atoms to form larger graphene solid sheets with induced energy bandgap. Unlike pristine graphene sheets which has zero energy bandgap, the as-formed graphene solid sheets are having intrinsic optical absorption and photoluminescence properties, due to its bandgap opened nature. In this interconnection process the quantum behavior of the GQDs is retained, even after becoming micron sized sheet like sample. Local structural discontinuity resulted around the metal atoms has helped to retain the GQD behavior in the solid sheet sample. At the same time the linker Fe atoms provided room temperature weak ferromagnetic behavior to the system. The present paper discusses the outcome of the characterization results, which demonstrate the energy bandgap opened nature and magnetic behavior of the GQD interconnected solid sheet system.

ce:section.ce:para: A dilute magnetic semiconductor (DMS) is a non-magnetic semiconductor doped with a small number of magnetic atoms, mostly transition metal elements. These materials exhibit ferromagnetism at room temperature. Though there has been a flurry of research in this field in the past few years, the origin of ferromagnetism in DMS is still a matter of controversy. DMS is of utmost importance nowadays, because of its prospective applications like spintronics, which require ferromagnetic ordering at room temperature. In almost all the DMSs, the ferromagnetism is carrier-mediated and this enables charge manipulation to modify their magnetic behaviour [1–3]. Spintronics is a field, where the spin degree of freedom of electrons is controlled to enhance the versatility of electronic devices. For example, by controlling the spin of electrons in an electronic device, information can be stored and transferred efficiently. The major advantages of the spintronic devices are; (i) information can be stored into particularly oriented spins and spins attached to the moving electrons are able to carry information through the electron transport and (ii) control of electron spin can be implied as a qubit in quantum computing.

Search for magnetism in graphene is a topic of great research interest due to its applicability in spintronics, provided the graphene has the semiconducting property. Pristine graphene is a non-magnetic material, due to the lack of unpaired spin and the consequent absence of localized magnetic moments. Various attempts have been made to induce magnetic property in graphene, such as vacancy creation, introduction of magnetic impurities, and adsorption of metal adatoms (which is mostly a theoretically studied topic) [4–7]. On the other hand, the nanofragments of graphene such as graphene nanoribbons and graphene quantum dots were reported to have magnetic properties, in addition to their semiconducting behaviour [8,9]. Magnetism in such graphene nanostructures were reported to originate from the spin-polarized zigzag edge states,[10] nitrogen doping,[5] vacancies and adatoms [11,12]. Recently, E. Bulut Kul et.al., theoretically investigated the magnetic properties of graphene quantum dots with two charged vacancies [13].

Quantum dots (QDs) are nanoscale clusters of semiconductive atoms providing confinement to the electron/hole motion due to their small size. One of the most important outcomes of this confinement effect is tunable bandgap with control on the light absorption and emission frequencies, and in this way, it becomes possible for one to have desired optical and electrical properties to be adjusted according to their purpose of use. The limitation of this material is the surface states which arise due to the high surface to volume ratio. Therefore, efforts are underway to prepare quantum dots coupled solids. The coupling process must be in such a way that the quantum behavior of the interconnecting quantum dots must be retained and at the same time surface to volume ratio must be reduced, so that the quantum property of the material system can be harvested by downplaying the surface states. Here graphene quantum dots are connected through metal atom binding. The presence of Fe metal atoms in the graphene network has done a dual role. At first, it helped to interconnect GQDs into micron-sized single crystalline type solid sheet with strong intrinsic blue emission behavior, and then these incorporated Fe ions also acted as the source of magnetic moments, and therefore it was possible for us to develop a newer type graphene-based nanostructure material with room temperature ferromagnetic characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and GQD solid sheets
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) and GQD solid sheets were prepared by the hydrothermal method. In a typical reaction for GQDs, 0.5 g of citric acid (TCI Japan) and 0.25 g of urea (TCI Japan) were mixed together by dissolving separately into 20 ml (each) of double distilled water (DDW). The final solution was transferred to a teflon lined stainless steel hydrothermal vessel and treated at 180 °C for 180 min. The hydrothermal vessel was cooled down to room temperature naturally and the sample solution was collected. For the GQD solid sheet samples, FeCl3 (Sigma Aldrich) was added and the remaining experimental conditions are kept unchanged (different concentrations of FeCl3, viz., 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, and 0.9 mole were used to give F1, F3, F5, F7, and F9 samples, respectively). The GQD sample was named F0, and all the mentioned sample codes are used as the same throughout the manuscript. All the samples were purified using the dialysis process.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was carried out using a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument at an operating voltage of 200 kV. The samples were coated on carbon-coated copper grids (200 mesh) and used for the measurements. UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence (PL) excitation/emission measurements were carried out using an Agilent CARY60 spectrophotometer and a Horiba Jobin Yvon fluoromax-4 spectrofluorimeter, respectively. The photoluminescence lifetime measurements were carried out by using an IBH time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system, and the decay profile was deconvoluted using IBH data station software V2.6. Raman spectra were recorded using a Horiba LABRAM HR instrument excited by a 514-nm laser and X-Ray diffraction measurements were carried out with an Xpert PRO PANalytical instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using the XPS-ion-pumped chamber of an ES-2401 spectrometer equipped with a source of Mg Kα radiation at a photon energy of 1253.6 eV. The obtained high-resolution XPS peaks were deconvoluted using the XPSpeak software version 4.1. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was carried out using a Bruker Tensor 27 instrument.

The electrophoretic deposition (EPD) of the separated solid sheet sample was carried out using a DC regulated power supply. Precleaned fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) substrates were used as both anode and cathode. The distance between the electrodes was kept as 2 cm. The deposition was carried out by applying 5 V for 5 min using the solid sheet sample dispersion. The deposited EPD film was dried at 60 °C for 2 h and then subjected to Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM, using Quanta-250-FEG instrument). Photocurrent measurements were carried out using a LOT-Quantum Design 150 W solar simulator with 1 sun illumination, attached to a metrohm Autolab PGSTAT302N instrument. The EPD-coated solid sheet film was used as a working electrode. The platinum wire and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. A 0.5 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte.

The soft X-ray absorption beamline (BL-1) of the Indus-2 synchrotron radiation source at Raja Ramanna Centre for Advanced Technology, Indore, India was used for the soft X-ray absorption spectroscopy (SXAS) measurements. The SXAS measurements were carried out in the total electron yield (TEY) mode, in an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) chamber with a base pressure of 5 × 10−10 mbar.

The density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the projector augmented wave (PAW)[16,17] method as implemented in the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) [18–20]. The exchange–correlation interactions were treated by generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE)[21,22] functional. Van der Waals interactions play a crucial role in the interaction between adsorbate and the graphene layer [23]. Therefore, to include the long-range interaction between adatom and graphene surface, the PBE functional with Grimme’s [24] D2 correction was used for all calculations. In this method, a semi-empirical dispersion potential was added with the conventional Kohn–Sham DFT energy obtained from PBE functional. In the present study, we utilized a 4x4 hexagonal graphene supercell containing 32 carbon atoms, which was allowed to relax until the total energy difference between the loops was less than 10-5 eV and the force was less than 0.02 eV/Å. A previous study has shown that the use of 600 eV cut-off energy and a 6x6x1 k-point grid was sufficient for calculations with 4x4 hexagonal graphene supercell [25]. Hence, in all the DFT calculations, we have used a plane-wave expansion cutoff energy of 600 eV and 6x6x1 Monkhorst-Pack [26] k-point grid for sampling the Brillouin zone. To accelerate the convergence, a Methfessel-Paxton smearing of the Fermi surface was employed with a smearing width of 0.2 eV. A vacuum region of 12 Å is used to avoid periodic image interactions between the atoms in the supercell.

The interaction between graphene surface and the adsorbate atoms are characterized through adsorption energy,
E
a d s
and is calculated by using the following equation:
E
a d s
=
E
g r a + X
-
E
g r a
-
E X

Where,
E
g r a + X
is the total energy of the graphene surface with adsorbate X (X = Fe, O or Fe, and O),
E
g r a
is the total energy of the isolated graphene, and
E X
is the total energy of the isolated adsorbate X.

The as-prepared samples were subjected to TEM analysis to understand the morphology of the nanostructures. The sample prepared with metal-free conditions has resulted GQDs, and the sample prepared with metal precursor has shown two different morphologies, viz., GQDs and larger sized graphene sheets. The TEM images recorded from GQDs and sheets samples are shown in Fig. 1 . The dark spots in Fig. 1a indicate the dispersion of GQDs with an average size of about 5 nm. The samples prepared by adding metal precursors have shown an increase in the lateral size from quantum dots to sheets. For example, the samples prepared with 0.1 mole Fe show quantum dots with a slightly increased size of about 10 nm (Fig. 1b). When the metal concentration increased to 0.3 mole and 0.5 mole, the lateral size of the quantum dots has increased to ~ 500 nm (Fig. 1c) and ~ 5 µm (Fig. 1d), respectively.

It has to be noted that, while the graphene quantum dot structures have resulted polycrystalline electron diffraction pattern as shown in Fig. 1e, the well-grown graphene sheets prepared with 0.5-mole metal concentration has exhibited a single crystalline diffraction pattern with double diffraction spots. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern given in Fig. 1f, indicates the double diffraction spots and this is due to the presence of twinning effect in the graphene quantum dot solid sheets [27,28]. In fact, the twinning in graphene solid sheets can be evidence for the bottom-up growth of solid sheets. During the synthesis, GQDs are formed first and to their edges, metal atoms shall bind. The same metal atom can also help to connect other GQDs as reaction duration proceeds, thus resulting in micron-sized graphene nanostructure, which is nothing but a GQD coupled solid sheet. In this interconnection process, a perfect alignment between GQDs is not possible and therefore there could be chances for the twining effect in the resulting graphene nanostructures as evidenced in the SAED pattern. Jing-Bo Liu et al.,[27] and L. Brown et al.,[28] reported the twinning and twisting observations in CVD grown graphene sheets. The increase in the lateral dimension of solid sheets with the increased Fe metal precursors indicates a fact that the Fe metal atoms bind to the edges of GQDs and helped their interconnection process to grow into micron-sized solid sheets. A further increase in the lateral dimension is not favored with a further increase in the Fe precursor concentration (beyond 0.5 mole). Instead, a thick solid sheet has resulted from the increase in metal precursor concentration. For example, the samples prepared with 0.7 and 0.9 mole Fe metal concentrations show the formation of thick solid sheets as represented in Fig. S1.

In our previous work on the Zn-metal assisted growth of solid sheets,[29] the thick sheets formed at the metal precursor concentration of 0.9 mole, but in the present case, the thick sheets were formed at comparatively lesser metal precursor concentration. XPS analysis was carried out on GQDs and the Fe interconnected GQD solid sheet samples in order to investigate the chemical composition and chemical structure of the as-prepared GQD solid sheet samples. The XPS survey spectra (presented in Fig. S2) of the samples GQDs and solid sheets show the presence of C, N, O, and Fe elements with a minimum amount of chlorine. The possible inclusion of chlorine in the GQD solid sheet samples could be from the metal chloride precursors. The high-resolution C 1s spectra obtained from the GQDs and GQD solid sheets (Fig. 2 a) were deconvoluted into two peaks as a strong peak at 284.6 eV corresponding to the graphitic CC/CC and a weak peak at 288 eV [30] corresponding to the CO. This indicates that the as-prepared GQDs and Fe interconnected solid sheets were more graphitic in nature with a minimum amount of oxygen functional groups.The calculated oxygen to carbon ratio is as low as 0.22 and which is comparable to the value of high-quality graphene samples prepared by CVD and other methods [31]. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 2b) at 399.6 eV were deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.7, 399.6, and 400.5 eV corresponding to the pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen species (as represented in Fig. 2e) [32–36] (for all the samples). The O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2c) at 531.2 eV contained a single symmetric peak corresponding to the CO [37]. The nitrogen atoms were introduced in three different positions in the GQDs and Fe interconnected GQD solid sheet samples. This is in contrast to the previously reported Zn interconnected GQD solid sheet samples,[29] where nitrogen atoms are mostly incorporated in the graphitic position. Whenever the graphene structure has more nitrogen doping at the pyridinic and pyrrolic position then local structural disorder around that particular nitrogen position will be there, and with which, we believe that the lateral size of the sheet is not able to increase much. It could be because of this reason the average size of solid sheets obtained using Fe as interconnecting atom results with relatively smaller sized solid sheets. The Fe 2p high-resolution XPS spectra given in Fig. 2f show the characteristic Fe peaks at 711.1 and 724.7 eV corresponding to Fe2p 3/2 and Fe 2p 1/2, respectively [38,39]. The elemental composition of GQDs and Fe interconnected solid sheets samples are tabulated in table T1.

X-ray diffraction reveals the crystal structure details of the as-prepared samples. The X-ray diffraction pattern recorded from the GQDs and Fe interconnected GQD solid sheets samples are given in Fig. S3. The diffraction peak observed at around 25° indicates the formation of hexagonal graphitic structure which is matching with the JCPDS card No. 898487, corresponding to the graphitic carbon. The broadness of the X-ray diffraction peaks indicates the formation of nanosized crystalline carbon. The d-spacing value calculated from the diffraction pattern of GQDs (F0) and GQD solid sheets (F1, F3) is 0.346 nm, which is slightly higher when compared to that of pure graphite (0.34 nm).

The GQD solid sheet samples prepared with higher metal concentrations (F5, F7 & F9) have resulted in higher d-spacing values of 0.37 nm. This observed increase in the d-spacing values could be due to the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups between the graphene layers [40]. Raman spectroscopy is a potential tool to characterize nanomaterials, especially the carbon-based structures. Appearance of D, G, and 2D bands in the Raman spectra has been a confirmation study to the formation of graphene structure. The as-prepared GQDs and Fe interconnected GQD solid sheets were characterized using Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectra recorded from the as-prepared samples are given in Fig. 3 .

All the samples have produced two characteristic peaks at 1355 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively [41]. It is well known that the D band originates from the A1g mode vibrations of defect structures of graphene and the G band originates from the E2g mode vibrations of the graphitic structure. The Raman spectrum of the GQD sample shows a G band dominating structure, which indicates that the GQDs are more graphitic in nature. The Raman spectra of GQD solid sheets prepared by the addition of metal precursors show an increase in the intensity of the D band. This defect band dominated structure could be the result of the metal-mediated interconnection process. In other words, during the interconnection process, the metal atoms bind to the edges of the adjacent graphene quantum dots to form a sheet-like structure and due to the difference in valence between the carbon and metal atoms, the metal-mediated interconnection process could not result in a perfect sp2 hybridized structure. As a result, around metal atoms, the sp2 domains will see a discontinuity. Such discontinuity helps to keep the GQDs' intrinsic photoluminescence behavior undisturbed and we will discuss the emission properties in detail later part of the manuscript. A point to be noted here is that the interconnecting metals can also bind to oxygen to improve the stability of the structure and because of this reason the D band mode is increasing with the increase in the metal precursor concentration. It also has to be noted that there is no shift in the D and G band positions in the Raman spectra of the as-prepared samples and this indicates a fact that Fe is not existing in the form of metal clusters. Normally either red or blue shifts in Raman band positions are reported from graphene vibrational modes, when it is in the form of composite with metal nanoparticles. Because when a graphene structure receives or donates electrons from/to other nanoparticles, then there will be a shift in the Raman band positions. The absence of such shift in our samples indicates a fact that the metal is not present in nanoparticle form, instead, they are distributed in the atomic form throughout the GQD solid sheets by partially sharing electrons locally to the interconnecting carbon atoms of graphene structure.

The UV–visible optical absorption spectra measured from the as-prepared GQDs and Fe interconnected samples (consisting of GQD and solid sheets) are given in Fig. 4 a. The optical absorption spectra show two strong absorption peaks at 234 and 334 nm for all the samples which correspond to the π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [29,42]. The π-π* transition originates from the CC bond and n-π* originates from the CN/CO bonds present in the GQDs and GQD interconnected solid sheets.

Fig. 4b shows the PL emission spectra of GQDs (F0) and GQD solid sheet samples (F1-F9). It shows that all the samples produced an emission band in the blue region with a peak maximum at 440 nm. The quantum yield values of the as-prepared GQDs and Fe interconnected sheet samples, which also contains GQD, were calculated by comparing the integrated emission intensity values of the as-prepared samples to that of quinine sulfate dye. The comparative graph drawn for the integrated emission intensity values as a function of absorption intensity is given in Fig. S4. The calculated emission yield value for the GQDs is about 18% and it is increased up to 31% in the case of the sheet sample. The fluorescence photographic image of GQDs and Fe interconnected as-prepared (F5) sample are given in Fig. S5, and which demonstrates the highly fluorescent nature of the corresponding samples.

We believe that there could be an energy transfer type interaction between the GQDs present in the raw sample and solid sheets, which is responsible for the enhancement in the emission intensity of the raw solid sheet sample. To get further insights into the energy transfer process, the raw samples were subjected to the filtration process and by which the solid sheets and GQDs were separated. The separated samples were then subjected to emission and excitation analysis.

While the filtered out GQD sample has shown an 18% emission yield, similar to that of raw GQD sample prepared without Fe metal addition, the filtered in solid sheet samples exhibited a low emission yield value of about 2–3%. A sudden decrease in the emission yield value tells a fact that the sheet alone samples cannot give strong emission when excited directly. Instead, when they are interacting with GQD there is an enhanced emission. The possible interaction between solid sheets and GQDs are energy transfer type in nature. This is confirmed by the excitation spectrum recorded from the filtered in solid sheet samples, which have shown a systematic redshift with an increasing trend in the spectral overlapping with the emission spectrum of GQDs as shown in Fig. S6. It is because of this reason, the emission yield of the raw solid sheet samples increased with an increase of solid sheet dimension. It has to be noted that, the emission yield increases for the samples prepared using Fe metal atoms up to the concentration of 0.5 mole. A maximum yield of 31% was possible. The emission yield of F7 and F9 samples further dropped to 18%, which is equal to the emission yield value of graphene quantum dots. Even though, there is a better spectral overlapping between the excitation spectrum of F7 and F9 samples and the emission spectrum of GQD, an emission yield of 18% was resulted. This decreased emission yield value indicates the absence of energy transfer interaction between GQD and solid sheets in F7 and F9 samples.

Moreover, the presence of Fe in the solid sheet can provide magnetic properties to the solid sheet and therefore this material can able to serve the diluted magnetic semiconductor role. The magnetic properties will be discussed later and before that, we tried to find out the chemical coordination of the Fe atoms in the solid sheet through X-ray absorption analysis. To determine the electronic structure of GQDs and GQD interconnected solid sheets, the as-prepared samples were first subjected to the soft X-ray absorption spectroscopy measurements using a synchrotron radiation facility. The SXAS measurements were carried out in the total electron yield mode at C K-edge, O K-edge, and N K-edges. Fig. 7 a shows the soft X-ray absorption (SXAS) spectra measured at C K-edge. The sharp peak at 286.3 eV is excitonic and it indicates the presence of quantum confinement effect in all the samples [44]. The peak at 289.3 eV is attributed to the 1s-π* transitions of CO. The broad absorption band beyond 293.8 eV corresponds to the superposition of 1s-σ* transitions of CC, COC, and CO [45]. It has to be noted that, this broad absorption arises from the 1s-σ* transitions get suppressed when metal concentration was 0.3 mole and more and this could be possibly due to the decreased contribution from oxygen-related functional molecules at the edges. In GQDs, the surface to volume ratio is high and therefore a large number of functional groups occupied at the edges can contribute to 1s-σ* transition. When metal precursor was added to the reaction the GQDs get interconnected through metal atoms and the formation of solid sheet leads to the decreased contribution from the edge attached functional molecules to 1s-σ* transition. The SXAS spectra measured at N K-edge is shown in Fig. 7b. The X-ray absorption peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.9 eV, and 401.4 eV originate from the 1s-π* transitions of pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen atoms, respectively [46]. The broad absorption peak beyond 407.0 eV is due to the superposition of 1s-σ* transitions of pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen atoms [47]. As discussed earlier in the XPS N 1s spectra, the nitrogen atoms occupy pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic positions in the Fe interconnected GQD solid sheets. The O K-edge SXAS spectra presented in Fig. S7 show a peak at 534.3 eV corresponds to the 1s-π* transition of CO. Besides, the broad absorption peak beyond 542.0 eV is due to the superposition of 1s-σ* transitions of CO and COC [45]. It has to be noted that, the suppression of 1s-σ* transition peaks in the O K-edge SXAS spectra is in accordance with the peak at 293.8 eV of C K-edge SXAS spectra, confirming the presence of a lesser number of functional groups in the larger solid sheets.

Fig. 8 a shows the normalized near-edge X-ray absorption fine structure (NEXAFS) spectra at the Fe K-edge of the solid sheet sample filtered in from the raw samples. In order to examine the ionization state of the Fe atoms in our samples, a standard metal foil (Fe) and Fe2O3 were used as a reference. From the NEXAFS spectra, it can be seen that the features of the pre-edge and post-regions of our samples look similar to that of Fe2O3 reference, which manifests the Fe3+ ionization state of the majority of the Fe atoms in the Fe interconnected solid sheet samples.

A closer look reveals a slight shift in the absorption edge towards the lower energy side with the increase in the metal concentration, which indicates the slight variation in the atomic bond lengths with an increase of metal concentration. Further insights regarding the chemical environment of Fe atoms are revealed by the extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectra, presented in Fig. 8b. Fig. 9 a shows the k2 weighted χ(k) spectra up to the k range of 9 Å−1 which is good enough for fitting the data up to 3 Å in the R space. For fitting the experimentally obtained data, a theoretically generated model has been employed using the pre-determined value of the corresponding atomic bond lengths obtained from the quantum chemical calculations similar to our previously reported results [29].

This model gives three consecutive FeC shells of nearby bond lengths and a FeFe shell, far from the carbon shells. Based on the above-mentioned structure the theoretically generated model produces the χ(R) vs R plots and corresponding scattering paths. Fig. 9b shows the experimentally obtained phase uncorrected χ(R) vs R plot fitted with the theoretically generated model of the samples. As it has been mentioned above that the Fe as an absorbing atom is surrounded by C and Fe atoms in different shells, the backscattering amplitude from the first two FeC scattering paths at 1.89 Å (C.N = 2) and 2.10 Å (C.N = 1) contribute to the 1st intense peak that appears within the R range 1–2 Å giving rise to a broader peak and the next FeC path at 2.41 Å (C.N = 1) and FeFe path at 2.77 Å (C.N = 1) contribute to the next peak which appears within the R range 22.8 Å. All the above-mentioned scattering paths are supposed to be on the same plane of Fe atoms due to the planar structure of the graphene sheet. It has to be noted here that in the χ(R) vs R plot no shoulder to the lower side of the 1st peak has been observed, manifesting no bonding of Fe atoms with atoms of higher electronegativity than carbon (2.55), like oxygen (3.44). The value of the EXAFS parameters obtained after fitting are summarized in Table 1 , which shows the variation in coordination number and the atom to atom bond distance with an increase in the doping concentration.

It can be seen that with an increase in the metal concentration, all the bond lengths remain almost the same and the corresponding co-ordination numbers remain almost similar to their crystallographic value for the Fe metal concentration up to 0.7 mole beyond which it suddenly increases up to a higher value by affecting their local surrounding. The lesser value of the Debye-Waller factor (σ2) for the nearby shells also suggests a lesser static disorder in the structure, which is an indication of the higher stability of our solid sheet samples. It can be concluded from the above observations that though the C.N of the paths increases significantly after a certain concentration, the value of the FeC bond length remains almost the same or the size of the graphene sheet does not change after the subsequent amount of Fe doping. The observed results indicate that Fe metal was surrounded mostly by the carbon atoms, meaning that, the Fe metal in our samples were distributed as atomic Fe. The presence of FeC bonds is an evidence to the proposed metal-mediated interconnection process. A chemical structure of Fe interconnected GQD solid sheets was proposed based on the X-ray absorption measurements, which is schematically represented in Fig. 10 . This observation is in correspondence with our DFT based quantum chemical calculations, which will be discussed later.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is cost-effective, but a potential tool to identify the formation of graphitic carbon and other attached functional groups. The Fe metal atom interconnected GQD solid sheets were subjected to the FTIR spectroscopic measurements and the obtained FTIR spectra are given in Fig. 11 . The formation of graphitic structure is indicated by the aromatic CC and CC stretching vibrations at 1595 cm−1 and 1418 cm−1, respectively [48,49]. The presence of carboxyl functional groups is identified from the peaks at 3500 cm−1, 1696 cm−1 and 1187 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH,[48] CO [50] and CO, respectively. The amine functional groups were also attached to the edges of the GQD solid sheets and are identified by the peaks at 3293 cm−1 and 1653 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of N-H groups, respectively. In addition, the peaks at 1352 cm−1 and 836 cm−1 could be attributed to the aromatic C-N stretching and aromatic C-H out of plane bending vibrations, respectively. From the FTIR spectroscopic observations, we conclude that the graphitic structure is formed with carboxyl and amine functional groups attached to edges of the GQD solid sheets [51].

Magnetic properties of GQDs and Fe interconnected GQD solid sheets were measured using the vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). Fig. 12 and Fig. 13 shows the M−H magnetization curves of the as-prepared samples measured at room temperature for an applied field of −15000 to 15000 Oe. While the GQD sample shows a paramagnetic behavior, the solid sheet samples gradually started to exhibit weak ferromagnetic behavior. Similar paramagnetic behavior is reported for bare and boron-doped GQDs [52]. The weak ferromagnetic behavior observed in the solid sheet samples could be originated from the Fe metal atoms that interconnect the graphene quantum dots into solid sheets. The as-obtained M−H curves show the presence of a paramagnetic component in the as-prepared samples. Therefore, the paramagnetic component subtraction is carried out for all the samples, and the resultant curves are included in corresponding plots. After paramagnetic subtraction, the saturation is attained in the high magnetic field region (shown in Fig. 13 b, c & d for F5, F7, and F9 samples, respectively), which indicated the weak ferromagnetic behavior of our samples. The magnetization (Ms) value for the GQD sample is 116.97x10-6 emu, whereas the solid sheet samples exhibit magnetization values of 152.39x10-6, 396.12x10-6, 349.11x10-6, and 280.48x10-6 emu (for F3, F5, F7, and F9 samples, respectively). The magnetization value is higher for the F5 sample and thereafter it is decreased. The reason for this observation could be correlated with the morphology of the solid sheet sample. In the case of the F5 sample, the solid sheets are thin and transparent and therefore magnetic ordering is much better thus resulting in a higher magnetization value. In the case of F7 and F9 samples, thick morphology is resulted due to the layer by layer stacking of graphene quantum dot coupled solid sheets and therefore magnetic ordering is disturbed thus resulting in fewer magnetization values in those samples.

The coercivity of the GQD sample is 28.24 Oe, and it is found to be 333.08, 58.72, 155.65, and 138.34 Oe for F3, F5, F7, and F9 solid sheet samples, respectively. The magnetization (Ms) and coercivity (Hci) values of GQD and solid sheet samples are presented in table T3. There are few other reports that attribute the weak ferromagnetic behavior of graphene quantum dots to the nitrogen doping effect [5]. In contrast, our GQDs display paramagnetic behavior, despite having an appreciable amount of nitrogen atoms, which tells us a fact that the observed weak ferromagnetic behavior from the solid sheets samples was originated from the interconnecting Fe metal atoms. As discussed in the XAS part, the Fe metal atoms mostly bind with carbon atoms and therefore the Fe metal atoms remain isolated from other metal atoms. The electron spins of these individual Fe metal atoms contributed to the weak ferromagnetic behavior of the Fe interconnected solid sheet samples.

The interaction of Fe metal atom with graphene structure was investigated by the DFT calculations. This will help us to understand the nature of the interaction, binding sites, and the interconnection process. There are four possible reactive sites for adsorption namely top, hollow, bridge, and edge sites of a graphene sheet. In the present case, the Fe atom is binding with the graphene sheet only at the top, hollow, and edge sites. Fig. 14 a & c show Fe adsorption at top and hollow sites, respectively. Fig. 15 a shows the adsorption of Fe metal atom at edge site of graphene, followed by the interaction of Fe metal atom with two graphene sheets, which is given in Fig. 15b.

The adsorption energy for interaction at the top site is calculated to be −2.898 eV. Moreover, for the hollow site, it is calculated to be −3.707 eV. On the other hand, the interaction of Fe metal atom with the edge site of the graphene layer possesses a comparatively higher adsorption energy of −5.246 eV. Thus, on comparing these three reactive sites, Fe metal interacts well at the edge site rather than the top and hollow sites. The optimized structure presented in Fig. 15b, reveals a fact that the Fe atom can effectively bind at the edge of a GQD and it extends the binding to other GQDs, to grow the graphene network adjacently. The interaction of Fe atom with two layers of graphene is found to have an adsorption energy of −4.832 eV, which is strong enough to establish the experimental growth. The magnetic moments were calculated theoretically by allowing the Fe metal atom to interact with pure (Fig. S8) and COOH functionalized GQD at different sites (presented in Fig. S9), and the obtained values are tabulated in table T4. The calculated magnetic moment values of Fe metal atom binding with pure GQD structure at H (hollow), BH (moved from bridge to hollow), TB (moved from top to bridge), and E (edge) sites are 2.29x10-20, −0.46x10-20, 2.35x10-20 and 3.30x10-20 emu, respectively.

From the above results, it is clear that when Fe is interacting with the most favorable site (edge site), the magnetic moment is higher compared to the other structures. Similarly, for the Fe interaction with COOH functionalized GQD structure, the magnetic moments are 4.08x10-20, 5.09x10-20, 4.04x10-20, and −1.75x10-20 emu for H (hollow), BH (moved from bridge to hollow), TB (moved from top to bridge), and E (edge) sites, respectively. In the case of COOH functionalized GQD structure, the highest magnetic moment was obtained for the BH site interaction. It is worth noting that, the above values are obtained from the graphene sheet attached with a single magnetic atom. The experimentally prepared sample will have a distribution of Fe atoms and their magnetic moment values can add up to give a high experimental value. The experimentally determined magnetic moment value of our sample is 396.12x10-6 emu obtained for the F5 solid sheet sample.

The solid sheets separated from Fe interconnected raw sample (separated from F5) was electrophoretically deposited (EPD) onto an FTO substrate and subjected to the photocurrent measurements. The as-deposited solid sheet sample was analyzed by FESEM and the results are shown in Fig. 16 a & b. Fig. 16c presents the photocurrent curve recorded from the solid sheet coating for an optimized deposition time of 5 min and it shows a photocurrent value of 50 nA. When the GQDs were overcoated onto the solid sheet film, the photocurrent value rises to a maximum value of 3 µA as shown in Fig. 16d.

This observed increase in the photocurrent generation is attributed to the FRET interaction between GQD donor and solid sheet acceptor, and which is in accordance with the enhanced quantum yield values observed from the PL measurements. The photocurrent observations indicate a fact that the Fe interconnected GQD solid sheets are behaving like a bandgap opened graphene system. Upon FRET interaction with GQDs, the solid sheet samples were able to generate enhanced photocurrent which is important to use the solid sheets as the active layer in the solar cell device.

In summary, we have successfully prepared a single crystalline type weakly ferromagnetic, energy bandgap opened very thin transparent graphene solid sheets by bridging graphene quantum dots with Fe metal atoms as inter linker. Since there is no energy gap in pristine graphene, this material cannot be used active layer in the optoelectronic device fabrication. But the graphene solid sheet has an energy bandgap and therefore can be used as an active layer in device making. It means we can interact with this material with visible photons. In addition to this property, the graphene solid sheet system is having an interesting property of weak ferromagnetism at room temperature. When these two properties are added up then one can able to use this material for spin transport-related applications in the future. One more interesting observation from this material is about the single-crystalline nature. This reflects a fact that the metal atoms are distributed in the graphene solid sheet in a periodic manner at least a few nanometers away. The well separated and periodical distribution of metal atoms in a nonmagnetic graphene environment provides a potential value to this material for quantum bit information storage studies in the future.

Ganapathi Bharathi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Devaraj Nataraj: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Oleg Yu Khyzhun: Data curation. Daniel T. Thangadurai: Data curation. Kittusamy Senthilkumar: Investigation, Validation. Murugaiyan Sowmiya: Data curation, Formal analysis. Ramasamy Kathiresan: Data curation, Formal analysis. Ponmalai Kolandaivel: Investigation, Validation. Mukul Gupta: Data curation, Investigation, Validation. Deodatta Phase: Data curation, Investigation, Validation. Nirmalendu Patra: Data curation, Formal analysis. Shambhu Nath Jha: Investigation, Validation. Dibyendu Bhattacharyya: Investigation, Validation.
